report_name: DragonForce Ransomware Hides C2 Traffic in Microsoft Teams to Evade Detection for Months

threat_actors.name: DragonForce
threat_actors.description: A ransomware group active since at least June 2023 that has evolved to use sophisticated techniques, such as abusing Microsoft Teams TURN servers for covert C2, to target organizations like a U.S. services firm.

attack_patterns.name: DLL Side-Loading
attack_patterns.description: The attackers loaded their malicious code using a legitimate executable (DbgView64.exe) to evade defenses.

attack_patterns.name: Exploitation for Privilege Escalation
attack_patterns.description: The attackers exploited a then-undocumented vulnerability in a Huawei driver to escalate privileges to SYSTEM.

attack_patterns.name: Web Protocols
attack_patterns.description: The Backdoor.Turn malware used web protocols (TURN, QUIC) to establish and maintain its command and control channel, blending in with legitimate Microsoft Teams traffic.

attack_patterns.name: External Proxy
attack_patterns.description: The malware abused legitimate Microsoft Teams TURN servers as an external proxy to relay its initial C2 connection setup, hiding the true destination of the C2 server.

attack_patterns.name: Windows Command Shell
attack_patterns.description: The Backdoor.Turn malware provided remote command execution capabilities, likely through the Windows Command Shell.

attack_patterns.name: OS Credential Dumping
attack_patterns.description: The Backdoor.Turn malware had the capability to perform credential theft from the compromised system.

attack_patterns.name: Network Service Discovery
attack_patterns.description: The Backdoor.Turn malware included network scanning capabilities to discover other services on the network.

malware.name: Backdoor.Turn
malware.description: A custom Go-based backdoor that abuses Microsoft Teams' TURN relay servers to disguise its command-and-control communications, making malicious traffic appear as legitimate Teams activity.

tools.name: DbgView64.exe
tools.description: A legitimate Sysinternals tool abused by DragonForce for DLL Side-Loading to load their malicious code.

vulnerabilities.name: Unpatched SQL Server Vulnerability
vulnerabilities.description: An unknown vulnerability in an SQL or MSSQL server was the likely initial access vector for the DragonForce attack.

vulnerabilities.name: Huawei Driver Vulnerability
vulnerabilities.description: A then-undocumented vulnerability in a Huawei driver was exploited by the attackers to escalate privileges to SYSTEM.

identities.name: U.S. services firm
identities.description: A U.S.-based services firm targeted by the DragonForce ransomware group in an attack starting December 2025.

infrastructure.name: Microsoft Teams TURN Relay Servers
infrastructure.description: Legitimate Microsoft Teams TURN servers abused by the Backdoor.Turn malware to relay and disguise C2 communications, making them appear as normal Teams traffic.

infrastructure.name: DragonForce C2 Server
infrastructure.description: The true C2 server that communicates with the Backdoor.Turn implant via a direct, encrypted QUIC session after the initial connection is brokered by a Microsoft TURN server.

indicators.name: Abused DbgView64.exe Execution
indicators.x_netsecio_sigma_rule:
title: Abused DbgView64.exe Execution
id: 9b1f3c4d-5e6a-4b78-9c0d-1e2f3a4b5c6d
status: experimental
description: Detects the execution of DbgView64.exe, a legitimate Sysinternals tool that was abused by DragonForce for DLL side-loading.
references:
  - https://www.securityweek.com/microsoft-teams-relay-servers-abused-in-dragonforce-ransomware-attack/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-18
tags:
  - attack.t1574.002
level: medium
logsource:
  category: process_creation
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    Image|endswith: '\DbgView64.exe'
  filter_legit_path:
    Image|startswith:
      - 'C:\Program Files\'
      - 'C:\Windows\System32\'
      - 'C:\Program Files (x86)\'
  condition: selection and not filter_legit_path
indicators.description: Detects the execution of DbgView64.exe, which was used by DragonForce to perform DLL side-loading. The rule attempts to reduce false positives by filtering for executions outside of standard paths.

indicators.name: Anomalous Outbound TURN Traffic
indicators.pattern:
title: Anomalous Outbound TURN Traffic
id: 8a0e9b1c-2d3f-4e5a-6b78-9c0d1e2f3a4b
status: experimental
description: Monitors for unexpected or high-volume outbound traffic on UDP port 3478 (STUN/TURN) from servers or endpoints that do not typically use real-time communication applications. This may indicate abuse of TURN servers for C2, as seen with DragonForce's Backdoor.Turn.
references:
  - https://www.securityweek.com/microsoft-teams-relay-servers-abused-in-dragonforce-ransomware-attack/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-18
tags:
  - attack.t1071.001
  - attack.t1090.002
level: medium
logsource:
  category: network_connection
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    DestinationPort: 3478
    Protocol: 'udp'
    Initiated: true
  filter_teams:
    Image|endswith:
      - '\Teams.exe'
      - '\ms-teams.exe'
  condition: selection and not filter_teams
indicators.description: Detects outbound UDP traffic to port 3478, characteristic of the TURN protocol abused by Backdoor.Turn. The rule filters out legitimate Teams processes to reduce noise.

indicators.name: Anomalous QUIC Session to Non-Microsoft IP
indicators.pattern:
title: Anomalous QUIC Session to Non-Microsoft IP
id: 7f8d9e0a-1b2c-3d4e-5f6a-7b8c9d0e1f2a
status: experimental
description: Detects direct QUIC (UDP/443) sessions to non-Microsoft IP addresses, which could indicate a relayed C2 channel like the one used by Backdoor.Turn after an initial TURN handshake.
references:
  - https://www.securityweek.com/microsoft-teams-relay-servers-abused-in-dragonforce-ransomware-attack/
author: cyber.netsecops.io
date: 2026-06-18
tags:
  - attack.t1071.001
  - attack.command_and_control
level: low
logsource:
  category: network_connection
  product: windows
detection:
  selection:
    DestinationPort: 443
    Protocol: 'udp'
    Initiated: true
  filter_microsoft:
    # This rule requires enrichment with known Microsoft IP ranges to be effective.
    DestinationIp|cidr:
      - '13.64.0.0/11'
      - '20.32.0.0/12'
      - '20.64.0.0/10'
      - '40.72.0.0/13'
      - '40.96.0.0/12'
      - '51.103.0.0/16'
      - '52.96.0.0/11'
      - '52.132.0.0/14'
      - '52.224.0.0/11'
  condition: selection and not filter_microsoft
indicators.description: A hunting rule to detect outbound QUIC traffic to non-Microsoft IP addresses, which could be a sign of the direct C2 channel established by Backdoor.Turn after the initial TURN relay.

relationships.source_name: Anomalous QUIC Session to Non-Microsoft IP
